topics.title: Sorting Algorithms
topics.story: Sorting is the single most-studied problem in computer science. From bubble sort invented in the 1950s to TimSort (Python/Java's default since 2002) — the journey shows how algorithmic thinking evolves. In interviews, you rarely need to implement a sort from scratch, but knowing why merge sort is O(n log n), when counting sort beats it, and what makes quicksort fast in practice separates A from B candidates.
topics.analogy: Merge sort: split a deck of cards in half, sort each half separately, merge them in order — like two sorted hands merging into one. Quick sort: pick a pivot card, put smaller cards left and larger right, repeat for each group.
topics.memoryAnchor: O(n log n) comparison sorts: Merge (stable, O(n) space), Quick (O(log n) space, unstable), Heap (in-place, unstable). O(n) non-comparison: Counting (bounded range), Radix (digit by digit), Bucket (uniform distribution).
topics.firstPrinciples: Lower bound for comparison-based sorting: Ω(n log n) (information-theoretic). Non-comparison sorts break this bound by exploiting structure in keys. Stability matters when secondary sort order must be preserved. In-place means O(1) extra space (ignoring recursion stack).
topics.bruteForce: Bubble sort / Selection sort / Insertion sort: O(n²) time, simple to implement. Insertion sort is O(n) on nearly-sorted input — this is why TimSort uses it for small runs.
topics.optimizedApproach: For general sorting: use Java's Arrays.sort() (dual-pivot quicksort for primitives, TimSort for objects). For counting occurrences in bounded range: counting sort. For sorting by multiple criteria: stable sort (merge/TimSort). For nearly-sorted data: insertion sort.
topics.whenToUse: Problem asks to 'sort and then...': interval merging, meeting rooms, H-index. Custom comparator needed: sort by frequency, sort by multiple keys. Partial sort needed (top-K): heap sort. Integer keys in known range: counting sort O(n+k).

examples.title: Merge Sort — Divide and Conquer
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":2,"lineCode":"if (l >= r) return;","variables":{"arr":"[5,3,8,1]","l":0,"r":3},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Start: sort [5,3,8,1]. l=0 < r=3 so we don't return."},{"line":3,"lineCode":"int mid = l + (r - l) / 2;","variables":{"arr":"[5,3,8,1]","mid":1},"phase":"DIVIDE","annotation":"mid=1. Split into left=[5,3] (indices 0-1) and right=[8,1] (indices 2-3)."},{"line":4,"lineCode":"sort(arr, l, mid);","variables":{"left":"[5,3] → [3,5]"},"phase":"RECURSE","annotation":"Recursively sort left half [5,3] → produces [3,5] in place."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"sort(arr, mid + 1, r);","variables":{"right":"[8,1] → [1,8]"},"phase":"RECURSE","annotation":"Recursively sort right half [8,1] → produces [1,8] in place."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"merge(arr, l, mid, r);","variables":{"arr":"[3,5,1,8]"},"phase":"MERGE","annotation":"Merge [3,5] and [1,8]: compare 3 vs 1→pick 1, compare 3 vs 8→pick 3, pick 5, pick 8."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"if (temp[i] <= temp[j]) arr[k++] = temp[i++];","variables":{"arr":"[1,3,5,8]"},"phase":"MERGE","annotation":"Merge with two pointers on temp copy. Result: [1,3,5,8]."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"// sorted!","variables":{"arr":"[1,3,5,8]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Array fully sorted. T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n) by Master Theorem."}]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION mergeSort(arr, l, r):
  IF l >= r: RETURN          // base case: 1 element
  mid = l + (r-l)/2
  mergeSort(arr, l, mid)     // sort left
  mergeSort(arr, mid+1, r)   // sort right
  merge(arr, l, mid, r)      // combine

FUNCTION merge(arr, l, mid, r):
  temp = copy of arr[l..r]
  i=0 (left ptr), j=mid-l+1 (right ptr), k=l
  WHILE both halves have elements:
    pick smaller element → arr[k++]
  copy remaining elements
examples.explanation: Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) in all cases — never degrades like quicksort. Two sorted arrays merge in O(n) with two pointers. Divide log n times, O(n) work per level = O(n log n) total. Stable: equal elements keep original relative order — critical for multi-key sorting.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Arrays.sort() for objects uses TimSort (merge sort + insertion sort). Python's sorted() uses TimSort. Database ORDER BY on large datasets uses external merge sort (data too large for RAM). Git uses patience sort (merge sort variant). Anywhere stability and worst-case guarantees matter: financial records, medical data.

examples.title: Quick Sort — Lomuto Partition
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"int pivot = arr[r];","variables":{"arr":"[4,2,7,1,5]","pivot":5,"i":-1},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Lomuto: pick last element (5) as pivot. i=-1 tracks the smaller-region boundary."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"if (arr[j] <= pivot)","variables":{"j":0,"arr[j]":4,"pivot":5,"i":0},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"j=0: arr[0]=4 ≤ 5 → i=0, swap arr[0]↔arr[0] (no change). 4 enters smaller region."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"if (arr[j] <= pivot)","variables":{"j":1,"arr[j]":2,"pivot":5,"i":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"j=1: arr[1]=2 ≤ 5 → i=1, no swap needed. Region [4,2] all < pivot."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"if (arr[j] <= pivot)","variables":{"j":2,"arr[j]":7,"pivot":5,"i":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"j=2: arr[2]=7 > 5 → skip. 7 stays in the larger region."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"if (arr[j] <= pivot)","variables":{"j":3,"arr[j]":1,"arr":"[4,2,1,7,5]","i":2},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"j=3: arr[3]=1 ≤ 5 → i=2, swap arr[2]↔arr[3]. Array: [4,2,1,7,5]."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"swap arr[i+1] with arr[r]","variables":{"arr":"[4,2,1,5,7]","pivotIdx":3},"phase":"PLACE_PIVOT","annotation":"Place pivot: swap arr[3]↔arr[4]. Array: [4,2,1,5,7]. Pivot 5 is in its final position!"},{"line":18,"lineCode":"return i + 1;","variables":{"arr":"[4,2,1 | 5 | 7]","p":3},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Pivot at index 3. Left [4,2,1] all < 5. Right [7] all > 5. Recurse on both sides."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["[4,2,7,1,5] pivot=5"] --> B["Partition: scan j=0..3"]
    B --> C["4≤5: expand smaller region"]
    B --> D["2≤5: expand smaller region"]
    B --> E["7>5: skip — stays in larger"]
    B --> F["1≤5: swap→[4,2,1,7,5]"]
    C --> G["Place pivot: [4,2,1,5,7]"]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Recurse left: [4,2,1]"]
    G --> I["Recurse right: [7]"]
    H --> J["[1,2,3,4,5,7] ✓"]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION quickSort(arr, l, r):
  IF l >= r: RETURN
  p = partition(arr, l, r)
  quickSort(arr, l, p-1)   // sort smaller side
  quickSort(arr, p+1, r)   // sort larger side

FUNCTION partition(arr, l, r):  // Lomuto scheme
  pivot = arr[r]          // last element as pivot
  i = l - 1               // smaller region boundary
  FOR j = l to r-1:
    IF arr[j] <= pivot:
      i++; swap(arr, i, j)
  swap(arr, i+1, r)       // place pivot
  RETURN i+1
examples.explanation: Partition places exactly one element (pivot) in its final sorted position, splitting into two independent sub-problems. Average O(n log n) because random pivots split roughly in half. Worst case O(n²) on sorted input with Lomuto (fix: random pivot). Fastest in practice for primitives due to cache locality — works in-place.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Arrays.sort() for primitives uses dual-pivot quicksort (Yaroslavskiy). C++ std::sort uses introsort (quicksort + heapsort fallback to prevent O(n²)). JavaScript V8 switched from quicksort to TimSort in 2019. Interview trick: understand pivot selection, partition invariant, and when to use 3-way partition for many duplicates.

examples.title: Counting Sort — O(n+k) Non-Comparison
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"int[] count = new int[max - min + 1];","variables":{"arr":"[3,1,4,1,5]","min":1,"max":5,"count":"[0,0,0,0,0]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Input [3,1,4,1,5], range 1-5. count[0]=freq(1), count[2]=freq(3), etc."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"count[num - min]++;","variables":{"count":"[2,0,1,1,1]"},"phase":"COUNT","annotation":"After counting: 1→2x, 3→1x, 4→1x, 5→1x. count=[2,0,1,1,1]."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"count[i] += count[i - 1];","variables":{"count":"[2,2,3,4,5]"},"phase":"PREFIX","annotation":"Prefix sums: count[i] = # elements ≤ value(i+min). count[4]=5 = total length. These become insertion positions."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"output[--count[arr[i] - min]] = arr[i];","variables":{"i":4,"val":5,"output":"[_,_,_,_,5]"},"phase":"PLACE","annotation":"Process right-to-left: arr[4]=5, count[4]=5→4, output[4]=5."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"output[--count[arr[i] - min]] = arr[i];","variables":{"i":0,"val":3,"output":"[_,_,3,_,5]"},"phase":"PLACE","annotation":"arr[0]=3: count[2]=3→2, output[2]=3. Continue until all placed."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"return output;","variables":{"output":"[1,1,3,4,5]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Sorted: [1,1,3,4,5]. O(n+k) time, O(k) space. Zero comparisons — broke the Ω(n log n) barrier!"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["Input: [3,1,4,1,5]"] --> B["Step 1: Count\ncount=[2,0,1,1,1]"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Prefix Sums\ncount=[2,2,3,4,5]"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Place RTL\nfor i=n-1 down to 0:\n  output[--count[v-min]]=v"]
    D --> E["Output: [1,1,3,4,5] ✓"]
    style A fill:#e8f4f8
    style E fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION countingSort(arr, min, max):
  k = max - min + 1
  count = array[k] all zeros

  // Pass 1: count each value
  FOR num in arr: count[num-min]++

  // Pass 2: prefix sums → positions
  FOR i=1 to k-1: count[i] += count[i-1]

  // Pass 3: place right-to-left (stable)
  output = array[len(arr)]
  FOR i = len(arr)-1 DOWN TO 0:
    output[--count[arr[i]-min]] = arr[i]
  RETURN output
examples.explanation: Counting sort breaks the Ω(n log n) comparison barrier by never comparing elements. It counts occurrences, computes positions via prefix sums, then places each element directly. Right-to-left pass preserves stability. Only works for integer keys in a bounded range [min, max]. Time O(n+k), space O(n+k).
examples.realWorldUse: Radix sort's inner subroutine (sort by each digit using counting sort). Sorting ages, grades, scores in bounded ranges. Computing frequency histograms in image processing. Bucket assignment in distributed sort. When k=O(n), counting sort beats any comparison sort with O(n) time.

examples.title: Heap Sort — In-Place O(n log n)
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"for (int i = n/2-1; i >= 0; i--) heapify(arr, n, i);","variables":{"arr":"[4,10,3,5,1]","n":5},"phase":"BUILD_HEAP","annotation":"Build max-heap bottom-up starting from last non-leaf (i=1). Heapify pushes smaller elements down."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"after heapify","variables":{"arr":"[10,5,3,4,1]"},"phase":"BUILD_HEAP","annotation":"Max-heap built: [10,5,3,4,1]. Root=10 is the maximum. Tree: 10→(5,3), 5→(4,1)."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"swap arr[0] and arr[4], heapify size 4","variables":{"i":4,"arr":"[1,5,3,4 | 10]"},"phase":"EXTRACT","annotation":"i=4: swap root(10) with last(1). 10 is in final position. Heapify remaining 4 elements."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"swap arr[0] and arr[3], heapify size 3","variables":{"i":3,"arr":"[1,4,3 | 5,10]"},"phase":"EXTRACT","annotation":"After heapify: new root=5. i=3: swap 5 with arr[3]. 5 in final position."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"continue extracting...","variables":{"arr":"[_ | sorted: 3,4,5,10]"},"phase":"EXTRACT","annotation":"Each extraction: O(log n). After n-1 extractions array is sorted ascending."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"// done","variables":{"arr":"[1,3,4,5,10]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Heap sort: O(n) build + O(n log n) extract = O(n log n). In-place O(1) extra space. Not stable."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["[4,10,3,5,1]"] --> B["Phase 1: Build Max-Heap O(n)"]
    B --> C["Heapify from i=n/2-1 to 0"]
    C --> D["[10,5,3,4,1] — root=max"]
    D --> E["Phase 2: Extract n-1 times O(n log n)"]
    E --> F["Swap root↔last element"]
    F --> G["Heapify shrinking heap"]
    G --> H{"Heap size > 1?"}
    H -- Yes --> F
    H -- No --> I["[1,3,4,5,10] Sorted ✓"]
    style D fill:#fff3cd
    style I fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION heapSort(arr):
  n = len(arr)

  // Build max-heap: O(n) — bottom-up is optimal
  FOR i = n/2-1 DOWN TO 0:
    heapify(arr, n, i)

  // Extract max n-1 times: O(n log n)
  FOR i = n-1 DOWN TO 1:
    swap(arr[0], arr[i])   // move max to end
    heapify(arr, i, 0)     // restore on reduced heap

FUNCTION heapify(arr, n, i):
  largest = i
  IF leftChild exists AND arr[left] > arr[largest]: largest=left
  IF rightChild exists AND arr[right] > arr[largest]: largest=right
  IF largest != i: swap, then heapify(arr, n, largest)
examples.explanation: Heap sort = O(n log n) guarantee (like merge) + O(1) space (like quicksort). Phase 1 builds a max-heap in O(n) using bottom-up heapify (most nodes are near leaves, each costs O(1)). Phase 2 extracts the maximum n-1 times, each costing O(log n). Not stable — equal elements can reorder. The sorted portion grows from the end while the heap shrinks.
examples.realWorldUse: Introsort (C++ std::sort): switches from quicksort to heapsort when recursion depth exceeds 2*log(n) to prevent O(n²). Java's PriorityQueue internals. Dijkstra and Prim's MST algorithms. CPU/OS process scheduling. Finding top-K elements in O(n + k log n). Required when O(1) extra space is a hard constraint.

examples.title: Insertion Sort — O(n) on Nearly-Sorted (TimSort's Core)
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"int key = arr[i];","variables":{"arr":"[5,3,4,1,2]","i":1,"key":3},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"i=1: key=3 (card to insert). arr[0]=5 > 3 → shift 5 right. Place 3 at index 0."},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int key = arr[i];","variables":{"arr":"[3,5,4,1,2]","i":2,"key":4},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=2: key=4. arr[1]=5 > 4 → shift. arr[0]=3 ≤ 4 → stop. Place 4 at index 1."},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int key = arr[i];","variables":{"arr":"[3,4,5,1,2]","i":3,"key":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=3: key=1. Shift 5,4,3 all right (3 shifts). Place 1 at index 0. Worst case O(n) for one element."},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int key = arr[i];","variables":{"arr":"[1,3,4,5,2]","i":4,"key":2},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=4: key=2. Shift 5,4,3 right. Place 2 at index 1."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"// sorted","variables":{"arr":"[1,2,3,4,5]"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Sorted: [1,2,3,4,5]. O(n²) worst case, O(n) when already sorted (0 shifts each step). TimSort uses this for runs ≤32."}]
examples.explanation: Insertion sort is O(n²) worst case but O(n) on nearly-sorted input (few inversions — each element travels very short distance). This property makes it ideal for small arrays and finishing sequences. TimSort exploits this: it identifies natural 'runs' of sorted data, pads them to MIN_MERGE (32) using insertion sort, then merges them with merge sort.
examples.realWorldUse: TimSort (Java Arrays.sort for objects, Python sorted(), Android, Chrome V8): uses insertion sort for sub-arrays of size ≤ MIN_MERGE. Online sorting (elements arrive one at a time). Nearly-sorted data: leaderboards with minor daily changes, append-only logs. Small array cutoff in recursive sorts: all major libraries switch to insertion sort below size 8-32.

problems.title: Sort an Array (Implement Merge Sort)
problems.description:
Sort an array of integers by implementing merge sort.

**Example:**
sortArray([5,2,3,1]) => [1,2,3,5]
sortArray([5,1,1,2,0,0]) => [0,0,1,1,2,5]

**Key insight:** Divide array in half, recursively sort each half, merge sorted halves. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.
problems.hint1: Merge sort has two phases. What are they? When does the recursion stop?
problems.hint2: Phase 1: recursively sort left half (arr[left..mid]) and right half (arr[mid+1..right]). Base case: left >= right (single element is sorted).
problems.hint3: Phase 2 (merge): use a temp array. Two pointers, one in each sorted half. Always pick the smaller element. Copy remaining elements.
problems.editorial: Merge sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) space (temp arrays during merge). Stable sort — equal elements maintain relative order. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n) by Master Theorem (case 2: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n).

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.description:
Merge overlapping intervals.

**Example 1:**
merge([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]) => [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
merge([[1,4],[4,5]]) => [[1,5]]

**Key insight:** Sort by start. If current.start <= last.end, merge (extend end). Otherwise add new interval.
problems.hint1: If intervals are sorted by start time, which intervals can overlap with the current one?
problems.hint2: Sort by start. Iterate: if current interval starts before the previous one ends, merge them (extend end to max). Otherwise, start a new group.
problems.hint3: Track the last merged interval. If intervals[i][0] <= lastEnd, update lastEnd = max(lastEnd, intervals[i][1]). Otherwise push the last interval and start a new one.
problems.editorial: Sort by start: O(n log n). Single pass merge: O(n). Overlap condition: next.start <= current.end. Merge by extending current.end to max(current.end, next.end). Total: O(n log n).

problems.title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
problems.description:
For each number in an array, count how many smaller numbers are to its right.

**Example:**
countSmaller([5,2,6,1]) => [2,1,1,0]

**Key insight:** Modified merge sort — during merge, count how many right-half elements go before each left-half element.
problems.hint1: Brute force is O(n²). Can the merge step of merge sort count inversions?
problems.hint2: During the merge phase, when you pick an element from the RIGHT half, all remaining elements in the LEFT half are larger than it — that counts are inversions.
problems.hint3: Modified merge sort: track original indices. When right[j] goes before left[i], add (left.remaining count) to counts[original index of right[j]].
problems.editorial: Modified merge sort tracking original indices. During merge, when a right-side element is chosen over left-side elements, ALL remaining left-side elements are larger → increment their counts. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Meeting Rooms II
problems.description:
Find the minimum number of conference rooms needed to schedule all meetings.

**Example 1:**
meetingRooms([[0,30],[5,10],[15,20]]) => 2
meetingRooms([[7,10],[2,4]]) => 1

**Key insight:** Sort by start. Min-heap of end times — if current start >= heap.peek(), reuse room; else add room.
problems.hint1: Sort meetings by start time. For each meeting, can it use an existing free room?
problems.hint2: Use a min-heap of meeting end times. For each new meeting, if heap.peek() <= meeting.start, the earliest-ending room is free — reuse it. Otherwise open a new room.
problems.hint3: Heap size at the end = minimum rooms needed. The heap tracks when each room's current meeting ends.
problems.editorial: Greedy with min-heap of end times: sort by start (O(n log n)), then process each meeting. If earliest-ending room is free (peek <= current start), reuse it. Heap size = rooms in use = answer. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Largest Number
problems.description:
Given a list of non-negative integers, arrange them to form the largest number.

**Example 1:**
largestNumber([10,2]) => "210"
largestNumber([3,30,34,5,9]) => "9534330"

**Key insight:** Custom comparator: compare `a+b` vs `b+a` as strings. Sort descending by this comparator.
problems.hint1: How do you decide which of two numbers should come first? Is 9 > 30, so 930 > 309 — yes. Is 3 > 30? 330 vs 303 → 330, so 3 > 30 in this ordering.
problems.hint2: Custom comparator: compare a+b vs b+a as strings. If a+b > b+a, place a first.
problems.hint3: Sort with the custom comparator, join. Edge case: if the largest number is 0 (all zeros), return '0'.
problems.editorial: Custom comparator: (a, b) → compare strings (b+a) vs (a+b). This creates a total ordering consistent with which concatenation is larger. Proof: this comparator is transitive — if a>b and b>c then a>c in this ordering. O(n log n * L) where L = average number length.

problems.title: Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)
problems.description:
Sort an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s in-place in one pass (Dutch National Flag).

**Example:**
sortColors([2,0,2,1,1,0]) => [0,0,1,1,2,2]

**Key insight:** Three pointers: low, mid, high. Swap 0s to front, 2s to back, 1s stay in middle.
problems.hint1: Three-way partitioning. Use three pointers for three zones: [0..low-1] = all 0s, [low..mid-1] = all 1s, [high+1..n-1] = all 2s.
problems.hint2: Initialize low=0, mid=0, high=n-1. If nums[mid]==0: swap with low, advance both low and mid. If nums[mid]==2: swap with high, decrement high (don't advance mid). If nums[mid]==1: just advance mid.
problems.hint3: Stop when mid > high. The key insight: when swapping with low, we know nums[low] is 1 (already processed), so we can advance mid. When swapping with high, nums[high] is unknown, so don't advance mid.
problems.editorial: Dutch National Flag (Dijkstra 1976). O(n) single pass, O(1) space. Three invariants maintained: [0..low-1]=0s, [low..mid-1]=1s, [high+1..n-1]=2s, [mid..high]=unknown. Stop when mid > high (no more unknown region).

problems.title: Sort Characters by Frequency
problems.description:
Sort characters in a string by their frequency (most frequent first).

**Example 1:**
frequencySort("tree") => "eert" or "eetr"
frequencySort("cccaaa") => "cccaaa" or "aaaccc"

**Key insight:** Build frequency map, sort by frequency descending, build result string.
problems.hint1: Count frequencies. Then sort by frequency (descending). How do you rebuild the string?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap for frequency count, then a max-heap or sort the entries by count. Repeat each character by its frequency.
problems.hint3: Bucket sort alternative: create buckets[i] = list of chars with frequency i. Iterate from n to 1, appending chars * frequency.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 5*10^5 | s consists of uppercase/lowercase letters and digits
problems.editorial: Count + max-heap: O(n + k log k) where k = unique chars. Bucket sort: O(n) — create array of size n+1, buckets[freq] = list of chars. Iterate from high to low frequency for O(n) total. String.repeat() for clean char multiplication.

problems.title: Sort Linked List (Merge Sort)
problems.description:
Sort a linked list using merge sort in O(n log n) time and O(1) extra space.

**Example:**
Input: 4->2->1->3
Output: 1->2->3->4

**Key insight:** Find middle (slow/fast pointer), split, sort each half, merge. Bottom-up merge sort achieves O(1) space.
problems.hint1: Merge sort on a linked list: split → sort each half → merge. How do you split a linked list in half?
problems.hint2: Find middle using slow/fast pointers. Split at middle (set middle.next = null). Recursively sort both halves. Merge sorted halves using the merge-two-sorted-lists technique.
problems.hint3: Merge step: use a dummy head node. Compare heads of both halves, attach smaller to result, advance that pointer.
problems.editorial: Merge sort on linked list: O(n log n) time, O(log n) recursive stack space. Bottom-up iterative merge sort achieves O(1) space. fast=head.next (not head) ensures slow lands at first middle for even-length lists — allows clean split.

problems.title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
problems.description:
Find the kth smallest element in an n x n matrix where each row and column is sorted.

**Example 1:**
matrix=[[1,5,9],[10,11,13],[12,13,15]], k=8 => 13

**Key insight:** Min-heap initialized with first column. Pop k times, push next element in that row.
problems.hint1: The matrix's sorted rows suggest a merge-K-sorted approach. Or use binary search on the value range.
problems.hint2: Min-heap: start with first column elements. Poll minimum, push the next element from the same row. Do this k times.
problems.hint3: Binary search alternative: search in the value range [matrix[0][0], matrix[n-1][n-1]]. For each mid, count elements ≤ mid. The kth smallest is the smallest value where count ≥ k.
problems.constraints: n == matrix.length == matrix[i].length | 1 ≤ n ≤ 300 | -10^9 ≤ matrix[i][j] ≤ 10^9 | Each row and column is sorted | 1 ≤ k ≤ n²
problems.editorial: Min-heap approach: O(k log n). Binary search approach: O(n log(max-min)) where count(mid) uses the column-sorted property to count ≤ mid in O(n). Binary search is O(n log(val_range)) and doesn't depend on k.

problems.title: Maximum Gap
problems.description:
Find the maximum gap between successive elements in a sorted version of an unsorted array in O(n) time.

**Example 1:**
maximumGap([3,6,9,1]) => 3
maximumGap([10]) => 0

**Key insight:** Bucket sort — divide range into n-1 buckets. Maximum gap must span across at least one empty bucket.
problems.hint1: If we sort, max gap is O(n log n). The constraint is O(n). Think about bucket sort / pigeonhole principle.
problems.hint2: Pigeonhole: with n elements in range [min, max], the maximum gap must be ≥ ceil((max-min)/(n-1)). Create n-1 buckets of this size — any gap spanning two non-empty buckets is the answer.
problems.hint3: Each bucket stores only the min and max of its elements. The max gap = max over all adjacent non-empty bucket pairs of (next_bucket.min - prev_bucket.max). Internal bucket gaps are ≤ bucket size and cannot be the maximum gap.
problems.editorial: Bucket sort / pigeonhole: O(n) time and space. Key insight: with n-1 gaps among n sorted elements, at least one gap ≥ (max-min)/(n-1). Buckets of this size guarantee the maximum gap spans bucket boundaries. Store only min/max per bucket — the intra-bucket gap is always ≤ bucket size.

problems.title: Wiggle Sort II
problems.description:
Rearrange array so that nums[0] < nums[1] > nums[2] < nums[3]... (wiggle sort with large/small alternation).

**Example:**
wiggleSort([1,5,1,1,6,4]) => [1,6,1,5,1,4]
wiggleSort([1,3,2,2,3,1]) => [2,3,1,3,1,2]

**Key insight:** Find the median (nth_element), then 3-way partition, interleave.
problems.hint1: Simple approach: sort the array, then interleave smaller half at even indices and larger half at odd indices. Why does naive interleaving fail?
problems.hint2: After sorting, split into two halves: smaller = nums[0..mid], larger = nums[mid+1..n-1]. Place larger half at odd positions (1,3,5,...) and smaller half at even positions (0,2,4,...). Crucially, fill from right to left to avoid equal element adjacency.
problems.hint3: Sort, find mid. For odd indices (n-1, n-3, ...): fill with larger half (right to left). For even indices (n-2 or n, n-4, ...): fill with smaller half (right to left). This guarantees no equal elements meet at adjacent positions.
problems.editorial: Sort then interleave from the right. Split at mid: smaller half = sorted[0..mid], larger half = sorted[mid+1..n-1]. Place larger half at odd positions (descending from end), smaller half at even positions (descending from mid). Filling right-to-left ensures duplicate elements in the median region don't end up adjacent. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Pancake Sorting
problems.description:
Sort an array using pancake flipping — at each step, reverse a prefix of length k.

**Example:**
pancakeSort([3,2,4,1]) => flip operations that sort to [1,2,3,4]
Output: list of k values for each flip

**Key insight:** Find max, flip it to front, flip to its target position. Repeat for n-1, n-2... 1.
problems.hint1: Think about selection sort: to place the largest element in its correct position, you need at most 2 flips. Can you generalize?
problems.hint2: To place the element with value i at position i (1-indexed): first flip it to the top (position 0) with a flip of size = its current index + 1. Then flip it to its correct position with a flip of size i.
problems.hint3: Iterate i from n down to 2. Find the current position of value i (maxIdx). If already at position i-1, skip. Else: if not at position 0, flip(maxIdx+1) to bring it to front. Then flip(i) to send it to position i-1.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"[3,2,4,1]","output":"valid sequence of pancake flips that sorts the array"},{"input":"[1,2,3]","output":"[]"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ arr.length ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ arr[i] ≤ arr.length | All integers in arr are unique
problems.editorial: Pancake selection sort: O(n²) time, O(n) output. For each size from n to 2: find max in arr[0..size-1], flip it to front (if needed), flip to position size-1. At most 2 flips per iteration × n iterations = at most 2n flips total (within 10n constraint). Each flip reverses a prefix — simulated with standard two-pointer reverse.
